notes: Key-pair mode embeds the PEM private key at `config.snowflake.privateKey` as a single string (full `-----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----` â€¦ `-----END PRIVATE KEY-----` block with embedded `\n` newlines). Same `snowflake` sub-object as password mode â€” no separate nesting.

authStrategy stays `service_role` for key-pair mode (same tag as password). The server distinguishes password vs key-pair purely by which credential field is populated â€” `password` vs `privateKey`. Only one of the two should be set.
Username is still required in key-pair mode (Snowflake binds the RSA public key to a user). The `password` field is omitted entirely â€” not empty string, absent from the `snowflake` sub-object.

UI exposes a `Private Key Passphrase` textbox for encrypted PKCS#8 keys. When the key is unencrypted, no passphrase field is sent. Expected wire name is `privateKeyPassphrase` (under `snowflake`) but unverified until a probe with an encrypted key is run.

UI also exposes the private key as a file-upload control (not a textarea); the client reads the file and sends contents as a plain string. There is no multipart upload â€” body stays application/json Connect-RPC.
UI-required fields: name, locator, database, username, privateKey. Optional: warehouse, schema, role, privateKeyPassphrase.
TestConnector accepts the identical body shape and returns 200 for reachable creds; CreateConnector then uses the same body and returns `{connectorId, name, connectorType}`.

GetConnector persisted shape: `connector.snowflakeMetadata.{username, role, schema, locator, database, warehouse}`. `privateKey` is never echoed (matches password-mode redaction). `connector.authStrategy` remains `service_role`.

Snowflake auth-mode UI tabs: Key Pair, OAuth (SSO), OAuth (Individual), Password. `service_role` is shared by Password and Key Pair; OAuth modes use distinct `authStrategy` tags (see sibling catalog files).
